coredata.dc:title: Versatile Dual-Emissive Boron-Nitrogen Co-Doped carbon Dots: Unlocking efficient electroluminescent Light-Emitting diodes with breakthrough EQE Approaching 7%

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as pivotal nanomaterials for advanced electroluminescent displays, yet their efficiency remains constrained by limited triplet exciton utilization. Herein, we report a boron-nitrogen (B/N) co-doping strategy to engineer CDs with dual emissions of fluorescence and room-temperature phosphorescence, achieving high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 86.41 %. The optimized blue carbon dot light-emitting diodes (CDLEDs) exhibit exceptional performance with a maximum luminance of 1,305 cd m−2 and a groundbreaking external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 6.98 %, surpassing the 5 % theoretical limit for fluorescent systems. Theoretical analyses elucidate that B/N co-doping establishes a robust electron donor–acceptor framework, which enhances spin–orbit coupling, thereby synergistically facilitating reverse intersystem crossing and triplet exciton utilization. Furthermore, integration of these CDs with phosphorescent emitters yields warm-white LEDs with a luminance of 20,120 cd m−2 and EQE of 3.69 %, setting new records for solution-processed CDLEDs in terms of brightness and efficiency. This work provides a dual-path exciton management paradigm, combining intrinsic triplet utilization and energy transfer engineering, paving the way for eco-friendly, high-performance electroluminescent technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis of B,N-CDs from precursors of PhB(OH)2 and mPDA. (b) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of B,N-CDs, referring with mPDA and PhB(OH)2. (c) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image, shows the lattice fringes of CDs at a scale bar of 2 nm and the diameter histogram of 200 nanoparticles. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum (d) and high-resolution B 1 s (e), N 1 s (f) spectrum of B,N-CDs.

(a) Solvatochromic PL spectra of B,N-CDs in varying solvents, ranging from aprotic (e.g., n-hexane, toluene, dichloromethane (DCM), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetonitrile (ACN), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF)) to protic (e.g., isopropanol (IPA), ethanol (EtOH), and methanol (MeOH)). PL spectra of B,N-CDs under varying excitations from in Toluene (b), THF (c), DMF (d), IPA (e) and EtOH (f), ethanol (EtOH), (e) isopropanol (IPA) and (f) methanol (MeOH). PL mechanisms of B,N-CDs in aprotic (g) and protic (h) solutions.

Transient PL decay spectra measured at (a) ns window under different emission wavelengths and (b) ms window under different excitation wavelengths of B,N-CDs in degassed DMF solution. (c) Room-temperature fluorescence (RTFL), low-temperature fluorescence (LTFL) and phosphorescence (LTPH) spectra of B,N-CDs in degassed DMF solution (time-gated window of 1–10 ms). (d) Time-resolved emission spectrum (TREmS) of B,N-CDs in degassed DMF solution. (e) Schematic diagram of the luminescence mechanism of B,N-CDs. RTFL spectra (f) and temperature-dependent PL decay (g) of B,N-CDs:PVK film.

(a) Density functional theory (DFT) calculations models with varying the graphitized core from non-graphitized BN to g1BN, g2BN, and g3BN, and functional groups of −H to –NH2, −NHEt, and −NEt2 in the edge. (b) Frontline molecular orbital distributions of g2BN-H, g2BN-NH2, g2BN-NHEt and g2BN-NEt2. (c) The energy levels of HOMOs and LUMOs. (d) Energies of S1, T1 and T2 states of B,N-CDs models.

(a) Blue LED device configuration based on B,N-CDs. (b) Energy level diagram and chemical structures of PEDOT:PSS, TFB, PVK and TPBi. (c) EL spectra under different voltage, (d) luminance-voltage-current density (L-V-J) and (e) current efficiency–luminance-external quantum efficiency (CE-L-EQE) of blue LEDs based on B,N-CDs. (f) Comparison of EQEs for reported representative CDs-based LEDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) WLED device configuration based on B,N-CDs. (b) Energy level diagram and chemical structures of PO-01 and CBP. (c) CIE chromaticity coordinates of W1, W2, W3 and W4 devices. EL spectra (d) of W1, W2, W3 and W4 devices, inset shows their photographs. Luminance-voltage-current density (L-V-J) curves (e) and current efficiency–luminance-external quantum efficiency (CE-L-EQE) curves (f) of W1, W2, W3 and W4 devices. (g) Comparison of the L max versus EQE of W1, W2, W3 and W4 devices with the reported WLEDs based on CDs.

head.ce:title: Versatile Dual-Emissive Boron-Nitrogen Co-Doped carbon Dots: Unlocking efficient electroluminescent Light-Emitting diodes with breakthrough EQE Approaching 7%

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rational design of B/N co-doped carbon dots, enabling dual fluorescence-phosphorescence emission with PLQY of 86.41%. Optimized blue CDLEDs demonstrate high device performance (L max 1,305cd m−2, EQE 6.98%), surpassing the theoretical efficiency limit. Concomitantly, phosphorescent emitter integration achieves warm-white CDLEDs (L max 20,120cd m−2, EQE 3.69%), while theoretical analyses elucidate synergistic triplet harvesting mechanisms. A dual-path exciton management paradigm merges intrinsic triplet utilization and energy transfer engineering, paving the way for eco-friendly, high-performance electroluminescent technologies.

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as pivotal nanomaterials for advanced electroluminescent displays, yet their efficiency remains constrained by limited triplet exciton utilization. Herein, we report a boron-nitrogen (B/N) co-doping strategy to engineer CDs with dual emissions of fluorescence and room-temperature phosphorescence, achieving high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 86.41 %. The optimized blue carbon dot light-emitting diodes (CDLEDs) exhibit exceptional performance with a maximum luminance of 1,305 cd m−2 and a groundbreaking external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 6.98 %, surpassing the 5 % theoretical limit for fluorescent systems. Theoretical analyses elucidate that B/N co-doping establishes a robust electron donor–acceptor framework, which enhances spin–orbit coupling, thereby synergistically facilitating reverse intersystem crossing and triplet exciton utilization. Furthermore, integration of these CDs with phosphorescent emitters yields warm-white LEDs with a luminance of 20,120 cd m−2 and EQE of 3.69 %, setting new records for solution-processed CDLEDs in terms of brightness and efficiency. This work provides a dual-path exciton management paradigm, combining intrinsic triplet utilization and energy transfer engineering, paving the way for eco-friendly, high-performance electroluminescent technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In this era of booming digital and information development, the demand for high-quality light sources in display and lighting technologies has become increasingly intense. Early light-emitting diodes (LEDs) based on inorganic materials (such as SiC and GaN), despite their advantages of low driving voltage and wide viewing angle, were hampered by low luminescence efficiency and burdensome spectral regulation.[1] Subsequently, organic materials for organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) achieved a major breakthrough, realizing efficient three-primary-color luminescence through regulating molecular conjugated structures.[2,3] However, issues such as high synthesis costs and efficiency decay have restricted their commercialization like shackles.[3–5] In recent years, quantum dot-based LEDs (QLEDs) and perovskite-based LEDs (PeLEDs) technologies have emerged due to their optically adjustable size and composition.[6–9] Nevertheless, the toxicity problems caused by heavy metals such as cadmium (Cd2+) and lead (Pb2+) contained in them have raised potential environmental concerns.[10] Amidst growing environmental concerns, CDs have emerged as promising candidates for next-generation LEDs by synergistically combining organic molecular characteristics with advantages of inorganic quantum dots.[11–14].

CDs, as a kind of zero-dimensional nanomaterial with carbon as the core and a size smaller than 10 nm, have been favored by researchers since their discovery in 2004 due to their simple preparation, no metal element doping, high stability, and good solution processability.[15,16] Building upon these merits, CDs uniquely bridge organic–inorganic hybrid characteristics: preserving molecular carbon frameworks while demonstrating inorganic semiconductor-grade charge mobility, defect tolerance, and thermal resilience.[17] Their commercial competitiveness is amplified by scalable green synthesis, cost-efficient manufacturing, and PLQY rivaling conventional metal-based quantum dots (QDs). Notably, the photophysical behavior of CDs stems from quantum confinement-surface state synergy, enabling multicolor emission tuning through precursor engineering and heteroatom doping strategies.

Adopting the multilayer stacked architecture characteristic of OLEDs and QLEDs, CDLEDs demonstrate exceptional process compatibility through solution-based spin-coating fabrication.Liu's team pioneered the first white CDLEDs employing CDs as emitters (EQE 0.083 %), albeit with limited efficiency.[18] Tan and Fan engineered three-primary-color CDLEDs via naphthalene diamine/citric acid precursors, achieving luminance intensities of 12–136 cd m−2.[19] In 2020, Edward et al. reported an edge-amine modified CDs (PLQY 70 ± 10 %) that enabled record-breaking dark-blue devices (L max 5,240 cd m−2, EQEmax 4.04 %), surpassing the PeLEDs containing heavy metals at that time and achieving a significant breakthrough.[20] Nevertheless, inherent limitations persist under spin statistics constraints: conventional fluorescent CDs harness merely 25 % of singlet excitons, imposing fundamental ceilings on internal quantum efficiency (IQE ≤ 25 %) and practical external quantum efficiency (EQE < 5 %). To date, there are only two reports on the realization of 5.6 % EQE red-light CDLEDs[21] via fluorescence and phosphorescence dual emission and 5.68 % EQE yellow-light CDLEDs[22] via thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF). Crucially, blue-emitter development confronts three fundamental barriers: (i) precision synthesis of stable deep-blue emitters (λem < 450 nm), (ii) breakthrough strategies for triplet exciton harvesting, and (iii) operational stability enhancement in practical device architectures. How to break through the efficiency ceiling of blue-light CDLEDs via exciton engineering has become the key to advancing CDLEDs towards practical and industrial applications.

Heteroatom doping engineering has emerged as a pivotal strategy for precisely manipulating the electronic configuration of CDs, enabling targeted modulation of frontier orbital distributions, exciton dynamics, and ultimately optoelectronic performance. Our seminal work demonstrates this through N-doping via o-phenylenediamine/malic acid precursors, achieving record-setting deep-blue emission (λem 415 nm, PLQY 60 %) with corresponding deep-blue CDLEDs exhibiting EQEmax 1.74 %.[23] Mechanistic studies reveal nitrogen's electron-donating moieties enhance π-conjugation density, facilitating optimized band alignment for efficient charge injection. Structural rigidity and enhanced spin–orbit coupling reduce non-radiative recombination and improve exciton utilization. Moreover, the energy level structure, frontier orbital distribution and relative spin dipole moment of CDs can also be effectively manipulated by changing the type and spatial distribution of doping elements (such as S, P and B), resulting in the achievement of phosphorescence and TADF luminescence characteristics.[24–26] Our recent breakthrough in positional isomer engineering employs o-/m-/p-phenylenediamine derivatives to precisely control dipole moments, yielding deep-blue CDLEDs with CIE coordinates of (0.15, 0.07) and an industry-leading EQE of 4.51 %, currently the highest efficiency closing HDTV Rec.BT.709 standards.[27] However, since the N-doped CDs exhibit only fluorescent emission characteristic, the EQE of the device is difficult to break the bottleneck of 5 %. Boron (B) doping has emerged as an effective strategy for tailoring the properties of CDs, garnering significant attention in recent years. Firstly, B exhibits a similar atomic radius and crystal structure compatibility to carbon (C), enabling its incorporation into CDs to effectively passivate defects and enhance optical performance. Secondly, the lower electronegativity of B compared to carbon induces a localized positive charge distribution around adjacent C atoms, creating a p-type doping effect. Distinct from n-type dopants (e.g., P, S, F), B suppresses non-radiative recombination pathways thereby significantly improving PLQY. Thirdly, synergistic electronegativity contrast (B < C < N) and matched atomic radii enable uniform doping and defect modulation.[28] Strategic integration of multi-dopant architectures (e.g., N/B co-doping) and exciton-confinement nanostructures may unlock triplet-involved emission channels, which is expected to achieve a breakthrough in the efficiency of blue CDLEDs.

This research innovatively proposed the synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) through a solvothermal method, successfully achieving pure blue-light emission at 430 nm with a high PLQY of 86.41 %. The synergistic effects of B/N co-doping induced modifications in the energy-level structure, endowing the B,N-CDs with dual fluorescence-phosphorescence emission properties. Remarkably, the fabricated blue-light electroluminescent device based on B,N-CDs demonstrated a breakthrough EQE of 6.98 %, surpassing the theoretical limit of traditional fluorescent efficiency (5 %) for the first time. Moreover, the warm-white LED device (warm-WLED) based on B,N-CDs exhibited an ultra-high brightness of 20,120 cd m−2, an EQE of 3.69 %, and a low turn-on voltage of 3.2 V, setting a new record for CDLEDs performance. This work not only provides a new material paradigm for environmentally friendly display and lighting technologies but also contributes an innovative doping strategy for triplet exciton manipulation, which is expected to promote the CDLEDs technology to a broader application prospect.

B,N-co-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) were synthesized via solvothermal treatment (210 °C, 8 h) using phenylboronic acid (PhB(OH)2) and m-phenylenediamine (mPDA) as precursors in ethanol, followed by silica gel chromatography purification (Fig. 1 a). The rational selection of PhB(OH)2/mPDA pair enables controlled π-conjugation extension. The precursor stoichiometry was systematically optimized, and the maximize photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 86.41 % was achieved at molar ratio of 5:1 (Fig. S1). It was identified as the B,N-CDs for subsequent characterization. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum spectroscopy confirms the coexistence of boron- and nitrogen-related functional groups in B,N-CDs (Fig. 1b). For instance, symmetric stretching vibration (νss) and asymmetric vibration (νas), deformation vibration (δ), in-plane bending vibration (β), in-plane rocking vibration (ρ) and out-of-plane bending vibration (γ) of C=C and C-H bonds from the precursors were identified. After the synthesis of B,N-CDs, the νss(B-O) signal at 1368 cm−1 and νas(B-O) signal at 1346 cm−1 from Ph(OH)2 along with the δN-H signal at 1604 cm−1 from mPDA were synchronously weakened, indicating the B(OH)2 and NH2 groups react, like dehydration, to form B-N bonds.[29,30] Meanwhile, the signals of Ar-H in 660 ∼ 880 cm−1 region and 3000 ∼ 3100 cm−1 region were significantly decreased, and the aromatic ring framework signal (C=C(Ar)) exhibited a significant shift. These results indicate the formation of a graphitized carbon core in the B,N-CDs.[31,32] Additionally, a distinct νCsp 3 -H signal appeared below 3000 cm−1, suggesting that the B,N-CDs were modified with alkyl substituents originating from the solvent ethanol. It is worth noting that after the synthesis of B,N-CDs, the νC-B and νC-N signals shifted, and a typical νB-N signal emerged in the 1,278 and 1,250 cm−1. Therefore, it is speculated that B,N-CDs contains both B and N co-doping in a mixed boron-nitrogen clusters like B3N3 unit.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization of the B,N-CDs revealed uniform nanoparticles distribution with mean diameter 4.22 ± 0.26 nm (Fig. 1c). High-resolution TEM image showed a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, indicative of graphitic (100) planes. In the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, the B,N-CDs exhibited a dominant (002) peak at 21.3 (d-spacing 0.42 nm), amorphous carbon matrix with heteroatom-induced distortion and secondary (100) peak at 41.5 (d 0.21 nm), confirming in-plane ordering (Fig. S2).[33] Raman spectrum displayed an ordered G-band at 1607 cm−1 and a disordered D-band at 1380 cm−1, with a large intensity ratio (I G/I D) of 2.79, indicating the high graphitization in the B,N-CDs (Fig. S3). These comprehensive characterizations protocol establish precise structure–property correlations essential for optoelectronic optimization.

The element content and valence bonding of B and N elements were further characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As depicted in Fig. 1d, the XPS survey spectrum displayed four distinct peaks at 191.1, 284.1, 400.0, and 532.6 eV, corresponding to B 1 s, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, with their respective elemental compositions of 10.38 %, 72.11 %, 4.87 %, and 12.64 %. Consequently, the element ratio of B and N is close to 2:1, which is related to the ratio of PhB(OH)2 and mPDA precursors. Moreover, the deconvoluted high-resolution XPS B 1 s spectrum was fitted with three peaks at 191.1, 190.6, and 189.9 eV, which could be attributed to B-O, B-N and B-C, respectively, indicating that the B,N-CDs contained these chemical bonds (Fig. 1e). Correspondingly, high-resolution XPS N 1 s was fitted with three peaks at 398.8, 399.9, and 400.7 eV, assigning to N-B, N-C, and N-H, respectively (Fig. 1f). Besides, high-resolution XPS C 1 s spectrum exhibited four distinct carbon species, with C=O at 285.9 eV, C-N at 284.9 eV, C=C/C-C at 284.0 eV, and C-B bonds at 283.5 eV, respectively (Fig. S4a), and high-resolution O 1 s spectrum revealed the presence of O=C bonds (531.2 eV), O-B bonds (532.3 eV), and O-C bonds (533.5 eV) in the B,N-CDs (Fig. S4b). Furthermore, the structure of B,N- CDs was characterized via proton and 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR and 13C NMR) spectroscopies. As shown in Fig. S5, the 1H NMR spectrum of B,N-CDs exhibits aromatic proton (Csp2-H) signals ranging from 5.5 to 9 ppm, and the 13C NMR spectrum demonstrated that signals in the range from 5 to 50 ppm are indicative of –CH2-/–CH3 group from the ethyl moiety. Additionally, numerous signals observed in the range of 120–140 ppm are assigned to sp2-hybridized carbons in the aromatic ring.

To characterize the photoluminescence (PL) properties of B,N-CDs, solvation experiments were performed with varying solvent polarity, ranging from aprotic solvents such as n-hexane, toluene, dichloromethane (DCM), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetonitrile (ACN), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), to protic solvents like isopropanol (IPA), ethanol (EtOH), and methanol (MeOH). As shown in Fig. 2 a, when excited by 360-nm light, B,N-CDs displayed a single emission peak in aprotic solvents with a full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of 59 nm, and the emission peak center was red-shifted with increasing of solvent polarity. Moreover, even when the excitation wavelengths varied from 300 to 400 nm, the PL peak of B,N-CDs in toluene, THF, and DMF was maintained at 405, 418, and 430 nm, respectively, showing the smallest spectral shift (Fig. 2b-d). In contrast, under the same 360 nm excitation, B,N-CDs exhibited typical dual emissions in protic solvents of IPA, EtOH, and MeOH (Fig. 2a). Specifically, the emission peak at around 430 nm was weakened, and a strong emission appeared at about 500 nm. Notably, the center of the two emission peaks did not change significantly with varying excitation wavelengths, but the relative intensity changed regularly (Fig. 2e-f). In IPA and EtOH solvents, the proportion of the first emission peak reached the highest under 340 nm excitation and then gradually weakened, which is consistent with the absorption properties of B,N-CDs (Fig. S6). The dual emission phenomenon indicates that B,N-CDs may simultaneously possess fluorescence and phosphorescence characteristics and can trigger room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) in protic solvents.[34,35].

Based on the rational analysis, we propose that the observed phenomena can be attributed to the following factors: 1) The excited state of CDs exhibits partial charge-transfer (CT) characteristics (as evidenced by solvatochromic experiments). Under polar solvent conditions, excitons with CT components, due to their small binding energy (E B,CT), tend to dissipate their energy through vibrational–rotational relaxation when the radiative transition rate (k r) is not significantly higher than the non-radiative transition rate (k nr). This occurs even when sufficient excitons undergo intersystem crossing (ISC) from S1 to T1. Nevertheless, small E B,CT were not always harmful, and it does help realize the spin-flip process, as evidenced by the progressively attenuated weak emission tail-like signal from 500 to 600 nm in DMF (phosphorescence supposing); 2) In protic solvents, despite their higher polarity, the newly formed hydrogen-bonding network effectively suppresses the quenching of singlet excitons caused by vibrational relaxation, thereby increasing the population of available “effective” S1 excitons; 3) Meanwhile, benefiting from the crosslinking hydrogen-bonding network, the rigidity of CDs increases, which gradually reduces the non-radiative decay rate from T1 (k nr T). Such mesoscopic interaction not only facilitates stabilizing the efficient ISC channel but also increases the phosphorescence transition rate (k r T), thereby enhancing the intensity of room-temperature phosphorescence (Fig. 2g-h).[36,37].

It should note that competitive hydrogen bonding between CDs-CDs and CDs-solvent molecules must be considered. Compared the PL spectra of CDs in neat-film with those dispersed in solvents exhibiting distinct hydrogen-bonding capabilities, the consistent emission peak at ∼ 430 nm and ∼ 500 nm confirm the coexistence of hydrogen bonding (Fig. S7). Studies reveal that lower α-value solvent (e.g., ACN) exhibits distinct behavior compare to the film, while high α/β solvents (e.g., MeOH/EtOH) maintain luminescence consistency, directly linking hydrogen-bonding capacity (via Kamlet-Taft parameters) to emission behavior.[38].

The dual emission characteristics of B,N-CDs were identified as fluorescence and phosphorescence through time-resolved photoluminescence experiments. As shown in Fig. 3 a, similar lifetimes of 10.4 ns and 10.3 ns were observed at 430 nm and 500 nm, respectively, through time-resolved PL decay measurements within the nanosecond (ns) window in degassed DMF solution, while no delayed signals were detected in the microsecond (μs) window at these two wavelengths (Fig. S8a), confirming they all originate from same compound and exhibit typical prompt fluorescence characteristics in the short-lived lifetime range. Additionally, multi-exponential decays with lifetimes of 4.05 ms, 26.34 ms, and 53.73 ms were detected at 500 nm (Fig. 3b, Table S1), and the decay characteristics remained nearly unchanged under different excitation wavelengths (375/400/420 nm), demonstrating its room-temperature phosphorescence properties, which is consistent with the previously reported characteristics of RTP carbon dots. [37,39,40] The low-temperature fluorescence (LTFL) spectrum at 77 K resolves two characteristic emissions in 422 and 490 nm, which aligned to the emission covering zone of the excitation-dependent spectrum measuring at degassed DMF (Fig. 3 c and Fig. S8b). Compared to steady-state PL spectra, the narrowed FWHM of LTFL implied the surface state-mediated charge-transfer (CT) emission feature. During the time-gated low-temperature phosphorescence (LTPH) spectrum measurement, the dominated emission peak gradually changes following the delayed time from 1 to 9 ms. The phosphorescence peaking at 516 nm vanished after a delayed 9 ms with a remaining small hill-like emission signal at 490 nm, consistent with the above result of multi-component lifetimes measured on ms timescale. More importantly, the dominant peak of the fluorescence emission shifted from 430 to 422 nm as the delayed time increased, implying that the fluorescence emitting zone consists of components with different timescales. To further validate our hypothesis, the time-resolve emission spectrum (TREmS) was carried out (Fig. 3d). Surprisingly, the signal observed in the fluorescence region exhibited a timescale even longer than that in the phosphorescence region, suggesting that the 9-ms-delayed emission signal detected above should be assigned to delay fluorescence (DFL). Considering the rapid decay rate of fluorescence (k p), the lifetime of delayed fluorescence should mainly primarily depend on the rate of reverse intersystem crossing (k RISC). However, since the RTP compound typically exhibits a relatively fast phosphorescence radiative decay rate, the fact that DFL, as an alternative competing pathway utilizing the “dark state” T1 excitons, demonstrates a longer lifetime than phosphorescence can be well explained. These observations collectively establish the radiative transition pathways in B,N-CDs (Fig. 3e). Blue-emitting fluorescence (430 nm) coexists with green phosphorescence (480–517 nm), while slow-decaying room-temperature phosphorescence facilitates concurrent blue delayed fluorescence through triplet-to-singlet exciton recycling, unlocking multiple pathways for exciton utilization.

Furthermore, B,N-CDs were blended with the host material poly(9-vinylcarbazole) (PVK) to fabricate a solution-processed film (B,N-CDs:PVK). Steady-state PL spectrum of B,N-CDs:PVK film reveals dual emission bands: a dominant fluorescence at 422 nm, and a broad shoulder peak at 508 nm attributed to room-temperature phosphorescence (Fig. 3f). Moreover, temperature-dependent luminescence lifetime experiments of the B,N-CDs:PVK film indicate that the phosphorescence decay lifetime decreases from 188.08 to 70.02 ms as the temperature rises from 80 K to 400 K (Fig. 3g, Table S2). This not only confirms that the RTP process of B, N-CDs is accelerated in the host-containing state but also demonstrates that more triplet excitons return to the singlet state through the boosted RISC and are subsequently released as DFL, highlighting the excellent exciton utilization pathway of these carbon dots.

Combining infrared spectroscopy and other experimental results, we have confirmed that m-phenylenediamine and phenylboronic acid can form B-N bonds and construct a B3N3 ring core structure via a dehydration reaction. This structure can further transform into B,N-CDs during the subsequent graphitization process. Our study reveals that the NH2 groups at the edge can undergo a dehydration reaction with the solvent ethanol to generate ethylamine groups (−NHEt) and diethylamine groups (−NEt2), thereby achieving precise control over the functional groups at the edges of the B,N-CDs. Based on these findings, we have established the theoretical model system for B,N-CDs as shown in Fig. 4 a, with regulating the graphitization degree of the B3N3 ring in the core dimension (from non-graphitized BN to g1BN, g2BN, and g3BN), and regulating the types of functional groups in the edge dimension (from −H to –NH2, −NHEt, and −NEt2).

The structures of these molecular models were optimized by density functional theory (DFT) calculations, which also determined the electron distribution of their highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) (Fig. 4 b and Fig. S9). As depicted in Fig. 4b, the LUMOs of g2BN-H, g2BN-NH2, g2BN-NHEt and g2BN-NEt2 are laterally distributed, while the HOMOs are longitudinally distributed. This is because of that the strong electron-withdrawing nature of boron and electron-donating properties of nitrogen create a spatially separated distribution of HOMO and LUMO. Such rigid conjugated structure and frontier orbital distribution are similar to the hallmark of boron-nitrogen-based multiple resonance thermally activated delayed fluorescence (MR-TADF: Within a rigid polycyclic framework, the incorporation of electron-withdrawing and electron-donating atoms induces opposing resonance effects, thereby facilitating the spatial separation of HOMO and LUMO) materials, which can effectively use triplet excitons to achieve a breakthrough in OLEDs performance.[41] Therefore, the short-range delocalization of HOMO and LUMO in B,N co-doped CDs model is conducive to the localization of excitons on the resonance nucleus, which minimizes the non-radiative decay path and enhances the radiative transition probability, resulting in high PLQY. The empty p-orbitals of boron atoms in CDs facilitate charge-transfer (CT) type transitions involving both π-electrons from carbon and n-electrons from nitrogen, where the subsequent spin-flipping of n-electrons during this short-range charge transfer (SRCT) enables efficient singlet–triplet interconversion that constitutes the fundamental mechanism for both RTP and DF.[42,43].

DFT calculations provide insights into the electronic properties of the molecules, including their bandgap characteristics. As shown in Fig. 4c, increasing the graphitization degree of the core structure leads to a continuous rise in the HOMO energy level and a decrease in the LUMO energy level, resulting in a narrowed bandgap (Table S3). This is consistent with the classical theory that an increase in CDs size causes redshift in photoluminescence.[44] Moreover, edge amine alkylation causes synchronous upshifts in the HOMO/LUMO energy levels. However, the electron-donating effect results in a more pronounced increase in the HOMO level, consequently narrowing the bandgap progressively. Notably, the g3BN-NEt2 model, with a diameter of ∼ 2.7 nm closing to the determined size of B,N-CDs in TEM analysis, maintains a 3.16 eV bandgap despite dual regulation. This bandgap meets the wide-bandgap requirement for blue-light-emitting materials.

In the time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations, we compared the ground state (S0), singlet excited state (S1), and triplet excited states (T1, T2, etc.) of various models and systematically elucidated the intrinsic relationship between the excited-state energy level evolution and the luminescence mechanism of B,N-CDs (Fig. 4d). Unlike the trend observed for S1, the graphitization of the carbon core structure (from g1BN to g3BN) and edge alkylation (from −H to −NEt2) did not significantly reduce the energy level of the triplet state T1 (Table S4-S5). The energy gap between T1 and S1 remained within the range of 2.5–2.3 eV, which is highly consistent with the experimentally determined phosphorescence emission peak at around 500 nm. Therefore, as the graphitization of the core structure from g1BN to g3BN, the energy gap between S1 and the higher-level T2 (ΔES1-T2) narrowed from 0.705-0.638 eV of ΔES1-T1 to 0.323–0.313 eV. This trend indicates that further graphitization or alkylation to reduce the S1 level, or utilizing higher-level Tn states, could potentially meet the energy gap requirements for TADF. Combining the changes in spin–orbit coupling (SOC) matrix elements, the SOC values for T1/S0, S1/T1 and S1/T2 in g3BN-NEt2 were 0.217, 0.642, and 0.298 cm−1, respectively (Fig. S10, Table S6-S8). Such high SOC matrix elements reveal why the RTP process can be efficiently achieved, and the DFL could be generated via the RISC process from Tn to S1. [45–47].

Additionally, we analyzed the molecular dipole moments (μ) of various model structures in S0, S1 and T1 states (Fig. S11). For unfunctionalized systems (e.g., g1BN-Hand g3BN-H), μ values in S0 and S1 states are negligible (near 0 D) (Table S9-S10), whereas the T1 state exhibits small but distinct dipole moments of0.031 D(g1BN-H) and0.718 D(g3BN-H). In contrast, g2BN-Hshows a significantly larger μ in the S1 state of 9.318 D, which sharply decreases to1.164 Din the T1 state. After edge functionalization with electron-donating groups (–NH2, −NHEt, −NEt2), the dipole moments were modulated across states. As shown in Table S11, the μ of T1 state reverts to values closer to S0, ranging from0 to 4.45 D, reflecting weak intrinsic polarity. In contrast, the μ of S1 state increases markedly to5–14 D, indicating strong charge polarization and separation upon photoexcitation. Therefore, in polar environments (e.g., protic solvents orPVKhost material), stabilization of the highly polar S1 state promotes efficient intersystem crossing to the T1, aligning with the observed room-temperature phosphorescence in both polar media and thin-film configurations.[46].

The fluorescence and phosphorescence dual emission characteristics of B,N-CDs have prompted us to exploit their applications in LEDs for the development of next-generation display technology. As shown in Fig. 5 a, B,N-CDs based LED device was fabricated using indium tin oxide (ITO) glass as substrate anode, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene-sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) as hole injection layer (HIL), poly(9,9-dioctylfluorene-co-N-(4-(3-methylpropyl)) diphenylamine) (TFB) as hole transport layer (HTL), B,N-CDs:PVK as an active emission layer (EML), 1,3,5-tris(N-phenylbenzimidazol-2-yl) benzene (TPBi) as electron transport layer (ETL), and a LiF/Al double-layered as cathode. The energy level structure of B,N-CDs was determined by cyclic voltammetry measurements (Fig. S12, Table S12). In addition, in the Tauc plots, B, N-CDs presents an optical band gap energy (Eg,op) of 3.21 eV (Fig. S6b), corresponding to the fundamental gap (Eg,fu) of 3.09 eV (Fig. S13). The energy level diagram showed that the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of B,N-CDs are close to those of the PVK, resulting in a small energy barrier for charge injection from both electrodes to the PVK host (Fig. 5b). The optimized thicknesses of the HIL, HTL, and EML were 20, 10, and 20 nm, respectively. The preliminary Atomic force microscope (AFM) tests confirmed that B,N-CDs:PVK film had a minor root-mean-square roughness of 1.71 nm (Fig. S14), indicating high uniformity and few defects, which is conducive to carrier transport. Under ultraviolet light irradiation, a bright pure blue emission with Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) of (0.17, 0.11) was observed from the B,N-CDs:PVK film, while almost no emission was visible to the naked eye from the PVK film alone.

As shown in Fig. 5c, the blue light-emitting device based on B,N-CDs exhibited an increased electroluminescence (EL) with the bias voltage, with a peak center located at 440 nm, which was slightly red-shifted compared to that of pure PVK without CDs. The optimization of CDLEDs device was summarized in the Table S13, and the concentration of B,N-CDs was determined to be 3 wt%, with the thickness of the EML layer set at 80 nm. Luminance and current density curves as a function of applied voltage for the optimal B,N-CDs-based LEDs were shown in Fig. 5d-c. The maximum luminous brightness (L max) of the optimized CDLEDs device reached 1,305cd m−2, and the maximum EQE breaks through to 6.98 %, with a high EQE of 5.96 % maintained at 100 cd m−2 (corresponding to a low efficiency roll-off of only 14.6 %). The extra exciton luminescent-decay pathway and recycling mechanism not only play a pivotal role in boosting device efficiency but also effectively mitigate efficiency roll-off caused by locally high-energy exciton accumulation. Compared with previous reports, the performance of this CDLED based on B,N-CDs has been dramatically improved, breaking the EQE 5 % efficiency bottleneck of deep-blue CDLEDs device (λEL < 450 nm) for the first time (Fig. 5f, Table S14), highlighting its groundbreaking significance as a milestone in the field.[20–23,27,48–51] To make a systematic comparison, N-doped CDs (N-CDs) were synthesized using mPDA as the only precursor under the same experimental conditions. The CDLED based on N-CDs exhibited a CIE coordinate at (0.16, 0.09), a decreased L max of 452 cd m−2, and a low EQEmax of 1.63 %. This apparent comparison demonstrates that the B,N co-doping strategy enables carbon dots to utilize triplet excitons efficiently, making it the key to achieving metal-free, EQE-breakthrough and low efficiency roll-off CDLEDs.

White light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) play a significant role in advancing full-color display and lighting applications due to their wide color gamut and excellent color rendering, low energy consumption and high safety factor.[52–54] The development of efficient WLEDs that can accurately reproduce object colors has been a subject of intense research in both academic and industrial realms for a long time. To date, substantial efforts have been dedicated to developing innovative light-emitting materials with pure and saturated emission, which is essential for wide-color gamut WLEDs.[55] CDs, owing to their excellent properties, have emerged as promising non-toxic, efficient, and cost-effective alternatives for WLED fabrication, garnering significant attention in recent research.[56] Generally, CDs-based WLEDs can be classified into phosphor-converted WLEDs and electroluminescent WLEDs.[57] The former category suffers from poor stability and severe energy losses, as white light is generated by dispersing CDs with different emission colors into a polymer matrix or combining a short-wavelength light LED chip with yellow or orange emission CDs.[58–62] While the latter rely on direct charge carrier injection into the light-emitting layer, indicating good color chromatics and device stability to realize white light emission with improved efficiency.[63] Here, the B,N-CDs with exciton-recycling properties exhibit dual emission characteristics at 430 and 500 nm, which is expected to be applied in constructing high-performance WLED devices.

As shown in Fig. 6 a-b, the architecture structure and energy level of WLED devices was similar to the blue LED devices using 3 wt% B,N-CDs:PVK as the first EML layer (EML-I), and phosphorescent emitter of bis(4-phenylthieno[3,2-c]pyridinato-N,C2′)(acetylacetonate)iridium(III) (PO-01) doped 4,4′-Bis(N-carbazolyl)-1,1′-bipheny (CBP) as the second EML layer (EML-II). Four WLED devices were fabricated by changing the doping concentrations of PO-01 and thickness of EML-II, for instance, 3 wt% PO-01:CBP 2 nm (W1), 3 wt% PO-01:CBP 5 nm (W2), 6 wt% PO-01:CBP 2 nm (W3), 6 wt% PO-01:CBP 5 nm (W4) as the EML-Ⅱ layer, respectively. The EL spectra of as-fabricated WLED cover the entire visible light window from 400 to 750 nm and their CIE coordinates ranged from (0.31, 0.28) to (0.44, 0.41), manifesting precise regulation from standard white to warm white region (Fig. 6 c-d, Fig. S14). The CIE color coordinate of the warm WLEDs with 6 wt% PO-01:CBP (5 nm) as EML-Ⅱ showed slightly changed (ΔCIEx ≤ 0.01; ΔCIEy ≤ 0.01) as the luminance increased from 1,000 to 15,000 cd m−2, leading to ultra-stable high-quality lighting (Fig. S15, Tables S15-S16). The additional triplet exciton utilization pathway in the CDs layer facilitates efficient interlayer energy transfer (from the CDs layer to the phosphorescent layer), thereby reducing spectral shift caused by recombination zone migration under high current density due to imbalanced carrier transport (A common issue in white-light devices, where high-energy exciton accumulation significantly alters the exciton recombination zone under high current density due to effects such as singlet-polaron annihilation and triplet-polaron annihilation, severely impacting luminescence and carrier transporting.). Thus, the RTP mechanism plays an indispensable role in ensuring the spectral stability of this white-light device. Fig. 6e-f depict the luminance and current density curves of the WLEDs under different voltages, and the current efficiency and EQE curves under different luminance, respectively. The W4 device exhibits an extraordinarily high L max of 20,120 cd m−2 and a record-breaking EQEmax of 3.69 % for ultra-stable warm white emission, significantly surpassing the current EQE and luminance of CDs-based WLED devices (Fig. 6g, Table S17).[18,22,50,51,64–66] In contrast, in the absence of B,N-CDs:PVK layer, the referring LED device prepared with only PO-01:CBP as the EML layer showed orange-yellow light emission, with CIE of (0.46, 0.44), and EQE of only 1.63 %, confirming the critical role of B,N-CDs in the construction of WLED (Table S15). These results not only collectively demonstrate the versatile potential of boron-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots design for diverse device applications but also provide clear future research directions for scientists in related fields.

We demonstrate the rational synthesis of dual-emissive boron-nitrogen codoped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) through precursor engineering using phenylboronic acid and m-phenylenediamine. Comprehensive structural/optical analyses coupled with TD-DFT calculations reveal that the B,N-co-doping configuration enables precise regulation of singlet–triplet energy level alignment, facilitating simultaneous fluorescence and room-temperature phosphorescence via enhanced spin–orbit coupling. The DFL and RTP observed through various steady-state and transient tests demonstrate that this strategy is the key to achieving the breakthrough in exciton utilization efficiency in carbon dots. The optimized blue-emitting LEDs achieve record-breaking performance with high EQEmax of 6.98 %, surpassing all reported CD-based blue LEDs. Furthermore, we pioneer a dual-emission-layer architecture for white electroluminescent devices, integrating B,N-CDs:PVK as blue emitter and PO-01:CBP as orange-yellow phosphor. This design yields warm white LEDs with an extraordinarily high L max of 20,120 cd m−2 and a record-breaking EQEmax of 3.69 %, demonstrating exceptional color stability (ΔCIE < 0.01) across 1,000–15,000 cd m−2. The work establishes a new paradigm for heavy-metal-free luminescent materials, providing a dopant engineering strategy for exciton utilization beyond spin statistics and technical guidelines for multi-layer CD-based LED optimization. These breakthroughs position B,N-CDs as transformative candidates for energy-efficient lighting systems and full-color displays, bridging the gap between quantum dot performance and organic semiconductor processability.

The B,N-CDs are synthesized by subjecting to solvothermal treatment, in which phenylboronic acid (PhB(OH)2) and m-phenylenediamine (mPDA) and are dissolved in ethanol within a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL), after ultrasonic treatment for 30 min and then heated under the reaction temperature of 210 °C for 8 h. The molar ratios of PhB(OH)2 to mPDA for the different PLQYs are 1:20, 1:15, 1:10, 1:5, 1:1, 3:1, 5:1, 7:1, 10:1 and 15:1 (Fig. S1). After cooling to room temperature naturally, the B,N-CDs are purified through silica gel column chromatography by changing the volume ratio (v/v) of methanol: Dichloromethane = 1:10.

Quartz glass substrates are putted into cleaning ultrasonically with ethanol and dichloromethane, then the cleaned substrates are drying in an oven at 120 °C for 30 min, 0.3 mg B,N-CDs were blended with the host material poly(9-vinylcarbazole) (PVK, 9.7 mg) to fabricate a solution-processed film (3 wt% B,N-CDs:PVK) via 2,500 rpm for 50 s, which were subsequently subjected to photophysical characterization.

The quantum yields (QYs) of the B,N-CDs were obtained using the slope method.[67] Five different solutions of 9, 10-diphenylanthracene were prepared in ethanol (QY = 95 %) and their absorbances were less than 0.1 at 350 nm. The QY was calculated using the following equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of HOMO and LUMO energy levels
ce:section.ce:para:
A conventional method of cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement is implemented to evaluate the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of B,N-CDs with a standard three-electrode system, composed of a glassy carbon electrode as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode (auxiliary electrode), and an Ag/Ag+ as the reference electrode with Fc/Fc+ as external standard. CV curves were recorded at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1 in nitrogen-saturated acetonitrile containing 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6, supporting electrolyte) and B,N-CDs. The HOMO and LUMO levels were calculated from the onset potentials of first oxidation and first reduction and by assuming the energy level of ferrocene/ferrocenium (Fc/Fc+) to be −4.80 eV below the vacuum level. The formal potential of Fc/Fc+ was measured as 0.36 V against Ag/Ag+ using CV measurement. The energy gap (Eg) of B,N-CDs was then deduced from the UV–Vis absorption on-sets of the longer wavelength. The HOMO and LUMO energy levels extracted from CV were calculated from the following formulas: [68]

Steady-state PL spectra were recorded by fluorescence lifetime Spectrometers (PTI QM-TM) equipped with a monochromatized Xe lamp used as the excitation source. All time-resolve PL decay and emission spectrua and low-temperature spectra were measured using Edinburgh FLS1000. PL quantum yield measurements for films were measured by Hamamatsu C11347-11. Ultraviolet − visible absorption was measured using a Shimadzu UV2600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Low-temperature-dependent PL spectra measurements were performed in the temperature range 80–300 K using liquid nitrogen cooling. Electrochemical experiments were measured on a CHI760E potentiostat. JEM-F200 TEM was used to investigate the morphologies of the B,N-CDs. AFM measurements were performed with a Bruker dimension Icon Scanning Probe microscope operated in alternating-current mode in ambient air. The Raman spectrum was measured using Laser Confocal Micro-Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw inVia). The FTIR spectra were measured using a Nicolet iS50 spectrograph. X-ray powder diffraction patterns were carried out using Al-Kɑ radiation (Bruker D8 ADVANCE). XPS measurement was conducted using the Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi with a monochromatized. NMR spectra were recorded on the Bruker AscendTM 400 MHz NMR spectrometer with tetramethylsilane (TMS) as an internal standard. The thickness of the different layers was monitoring by INFICON SQC-310C deposition controller. The luminance-current density–voltage characteristics were collected by using a Keithley 2400 picoammeter. The absolute EL power spectra of the devices were collected using an integrating sphere and an PR650 spectrometer.

All the optical properties of different kinds of model B,N-CDs were calculated using time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) method as implemented in the Gaussian 16 program package.[69] The 6-31G* basis set was selected to combine with the functional B3LYP throughout all calculations (B3LYP/6-31G*). The excited state was optimized in vacuum to calculate the emission energy, which is the energy difference between the ground and the first excited state. The bule and violet colors in the HOMO and LUMO molecular orbitals represent the positive and negative phases of the molecular orbital wavefunctions.

The indium tin oxide (ITO) glass substrates are swabbed with Decon-90 solution, then putted into cleaning ultrasonically with ethanol and deionized water. The solvent on the surface is removed with dry N2 flow, the cleaned substrates are drying in an oven at 120 °C for 120 min, then treated with UV-ozone for 30 min to reduce the surface resistance and obtain ITO with a high work function. The poly(3,4 ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) is spin-coated at 5,000 rpm for 30 s on the ITO substrate to prepare a hole injection layer. After annealed in a heating platform at 150 °C for 30 min, the thickness is approximately 20 nm. Next, a poly(9,9-dioctylfluorene-co-N-(4-(3-methylpropyl)) diphenylamine) (TFB) is also spin-coated at 5,000 rpm for 30 s as hole transport layer, later the B,N-CDs (0.3 mg) blending with poly(N-vinyl carbazole) (PVK) (9.7 mg) dissolved in o-dichlorobenzene (2 mL) is spin coated at 2,500 rpm for 50 s and dried on a hot plate at 150 °C for 30 min, thus forming the emissive layer of the B,N-CDs. The doping ratio of B,N-CDs in the poly(N-vinyl carbazole) (PVK) is 3 wt%. After that, the substrates are transferred into the vacuum evaporation chamber, the 1,3,5-tris(N-phenylbenzimidazol-2-yl) benzene (TPBi) with different thickness (60–100 nm) are thermally deposited at a rate of 0.5–1.0 Å s−1 by thermal evaporation in a deposition chamber with a base vacuum of 8.5 × 10-5 Torr. Subsequently, a double-layer cathode is deposited, using LiF and al with thicknesses of 1 nm and 100 nm, respectively.
